title-group.article-title: Foundations of Quaternionic Quantum Computing: Qubits, Spinors, and SU(2) Geometry

abstract.p: We develop the foundational framework for quaternionic quantum computing, establishing rigorous connections between quaternion algebra, spinor representations, and the geometry of SU(2). We show that the quaternionic qubit—a unit quaternion in the Bloch sphere interpretation—provides a natural geometric encoding of two-level quantum systems, and that quaternion multiplication corresponds directly to SU(2) rotations on the Bloch sphere. We derive the quaternionic analogues of standard quantum gates, prove that any SU(2) gate can be expressed as a unit quaternion, and demonstrate that the double-cover relationship between SU(2) and SO(3) has direct implications for phase accumulation in quaternionic circuits. We further examine the quaternionic Hilbert space structure and discuss how non-commutativity of quaternion multiplication constrains the design of multi-qubit systems. This paper establishes the mathematical foundation for the subsequent papers in the RQM quaternionic quantum computing series.

license.license-p: This is an open-access article distributed under the terms of the Creative Commons Attribution License (CC BY 4.0), which permits unrestricted use, distribution, and reproduction in any medium, provided the original work is properly cited.

sec.title: 1. Introduction
sec.p: Quantum computing conventionally formulates quantum states as vectors in a complex Hilbert space, with unitary evolution described by complex matrix groups. However, the underlying geometry of single-qubit systems is more naturally captured by the quaternion algebra H, whose unit elements form the group Sp(1) isomorphic to SU(2).

This paper develops the foundational theory of quaternionic quantum computing, providing precise mathematical definitions and key theorems connecting quaternion algebra, spinor representations, and Bloch sphere geometry. The goal is to establish a rigorous foundation for the RQM quaternionic quantum computing paper series.

sec.title: 2.1 Definition and Basic Properties
sec.p: Definition 1 (Quaternion Algebra). The quaternion algebra H is the four-dimensional associative algebra over R with basis {1,i,j,k} satisfying i2=j2=k2=ijk=-1.

sec.title: 2.2 The Quaternionic Qubit
sec.p: Definition 2 (Quaternionic Qubit). A quaternionic qubit is a unit quaternion q∈H with |q|=1, representing a point on the 3-sphere S3.

sec.title: 3.1 The Quaternion–SU(2) Isomorphism
sec.p: Theorem 1 (Quaternion–SU(2) Isomorphism). The group of unit quaternions Sp(1) is isomorphic to SU(2) as a Lie group via the map ϕ(a+bi+cj+dk)=(a+dib+ci-b+cia-di).

sec.title: 3.2 Double Cover of SO(3)
sec.p: Theorem 2 (Double Cover of SO(3)). There exists a surjective Lie group homomorphism π:Sp(1)→SO(3) with kernel {+1,-1}.

sec.title: 4. Quaternionic Quantum Gates
sec.p: Under the isomorphism of Theorem 1, single-qubit quantum gates correspond to unit quaternions. The standard Pauli gates are represented by the basis quaternion units.

sec.title: 6. Conclusion and Outlook
sec.p: We have established the foundational mathematical framework for quaternionic quantum computing, demonstrating the precise correspondence between unit quaternions, SU(2) rotations, and single-qubit quantum gates. The non-commutativity of quaternion multiplication is shown to be directly relevant to the design of quantum circuits. Future papers in this series will build on these foundations to develop multi-qubit systems, quaternionic error correction, and applications to quantum simulation.

ref.mixed-citation: Hamilton, W.R. On Quaternions; or on a new System of Imaginaries in Algebra. Philosophical Magazine. 1844; 25:489–495.
Adler, S.L. Quaternionic Quantum Mechanics and Quantum Fields. Oxford University Press; 1995. ISBN 978-0195066432.
Nielsen, M.A. and Chuang, I.L. Quantum Computation and Quantum Information. Cambridge University Press; 10th Anniversary edition, 2010. ISBN 978-1107002173.
